1: Ps 76: Asaph's Zion Victory Hymn — Sennacherib's Shadow. This psalm is widely connected to YHWH's deliverance of Jerusalem from Sennacherib's Assyrian army (701 BC), when 185,000 Assyrian soldiers were struck dead overnight (2 Kings 19:35-36; Isaiah 37:36-37). Whether or not the specific historical occasion is Sennacherib, the psalm celebrates a decisive divine military victory that took place at or near Jerusalem. 'God is known in Judah; his name is great in Israel' — noda (is known, Niphal perfect) — the divine knowledge spreads through the victory: YHWH's name becomes great because of what he has done.
2: 'His abode has been established in Salem, his dwelling place in Zion' — Salem is the earliest form of the name Jerusalem: Melchizedek was 'king of Salem' in Genesis 14:18, and the Amarna letters refer to the city as Urusalem. The shortened form Salem appears here and in Psalms 76:2 as a poetic abbreviation. YHWH's sukko (tent/tabernacle/booth) in Salem — the divine dwelling evokes both the wilderness Tent of Meeting and Solomon's temple. The spatial claim: Jerusalem is where YHWH has chosen to place his name (Deuteronomy 12:5).
3: 'There he broke the flashing arrows, the shield, the sword, and the weapons of war' — 'there' (sham) — the specific location of the divine weapons-destruction. The list: reshpe qeshet (flashing arrows/coals of the bow), shield (magen), sword (cherev), and war (milchamah) — a comprehensive military arsenal reduced to nothing by divine action. The 'breaking' of weapons at the divine dwelling is the enactment of the eschatological peace vision of Isaiah 2:4/Micah 4:3 ('they shall beat their swords into plowshares').
4: 'Glorious are you, more majestic than the mountains full of prey' — naor attah adir meharerei taraph — 'more majestic than the mountains of prey/plunder.' The 'mountains of prey' (harei taraph) may refer to the mountains where predatory animals (lions, wolves) take their prey — YHWH surpasses even the most fearsome natural power. Alternatively, if referring to enemy mountains (Assyria's mountain-gods, or the mountain fortresses of surrounding powers), YHWH's glory exceeds their military power.
5: 'The stouthearted were stripped of their spoil; they sank into sleep; none of the warriors could use his hands' — the overnight destruction of the enemy force: 'sank into sleep' (namu shnatam) — the sleep of death, the sleep that does not end. The warriors' inability to use their hands is the divine disabling of military capability. This matches the 2 Kings 19:35 account: the Assyrians were found dead in the morning. The ANE military imagery of stripping armor from fallen enemies is the background for 'stripped of their spoil.'
6: 'At your rebuke, O God of Jacob, both rider and horse lay stunned' — gearatchah (your rebuke) — the divine rebuke as the weapon that overwhelms the enemy. The same gaar (rebuke) used of the cosmic waters (Psalms 104:7), the enemies of the cosmos (Psalms 80:16), and the storm forces (Psalms 106:9). YHWH's rebuke is a word-weapon that needs no sword. 'Horse and rider lay stunned/in deep sleep' (nirdam, a cognate of the sleep of death) — echoes Exodus 15:1 ('horse and rider he has thrown into the sea'): the Exodus pattern repeated at Jerusalem.
7: 'But you, you are to be feared! Who can stand before you when once your anger is roused?' — the rhetorical question of divine invincibility. Mi ya'amod lefanechah (who can stand before you?) — the divine panim (face/presence) before which no human or cosmic power can maintain standing. This formula echoes the Sinai theophany where Israel could not stand before YHWH and had to retreat (Exodus 20:18-19). The rhetorical question expects the answer: no one.
8: 'From the heavens you uttered judgment; the earth feared and was still' — the cosmic court: divine judgment descends from the heavenly tribunal to the earthly realm. 'The earth feared and was still' (haaretz yareah vashaqatah) — the earth's response to divine judgment is reverential silence. ANE divine court proceedings similarly depict cosmic silence when the great god speaks: the earth's stillness is the creaturely response to the creator's decree.
9: 'When God arose to establish judgment, to save all the humble of the earth' — the divine arising from the heavenly throne to execute judgment. 'The humble of the earth' (anavei aretz) — the anavim (humble, lowly, afflicted) are the protected class in the covenant: the widows, orphans, sojourners, and economically vulnerable whom YHWH specifically defends (Psalms 72:4; Isaiah 11:4; Zephaniah 3:12). The divine arising produces two simultaneous effects: judgment on the wicked and salvation for the humble.
10: 'Surely the wrath of man shall praise you; the remnant of wrath you will put on like a belt' — a difficult verse. 'The wrath of man shall praise you' — human anger and wickedness are forced into divine service: even the enemy's aggression becomes an occasion for YHWH's glory. The 'remnant of wrath' (she'erit chemot) that YHWH 'puts on like a belt' — the leftover fury of the defeated enemy is harnessed by YHWH, who girds himself with even the residue of human rage. The sovereignty of YHWH incorporates even hostile human will into his purposes.
11: 'Make your vows to the LORD your God and perform them; let all around him bring gifts to him who is to be feared' — the call to covenant response: the nations surrounding YHWH's territory should bring tribute (shay, gift/tribute) to the one who is awesome (lamora, to the Feared One). ANE vassal states brought annual tribute to the great king who had demonstrated his superiority. Ps 76 applies this political theology to YHWH: the divine victory over the enemy requires the tribute-response of all surrounding peoples. The 'vow' (neder) + fulfillment (shallemu) is the covenant-grateful response to rescue.
12: 'He cuts off the spirit of princes; he is feared by the kings of the earth' — the final divine act: yivtzor ruach negidim (he clips/cuts off the spirit of princes/leaders). The 'spirit' of rulers is their executive capacity, their will to act — YHWH cuts it off, rendering them incapable. 'Feared by the kings of the earth' (nora lemalkhei aretz) — the psalm closes with the universal scope of YHWH's sovereignty: the kings who brought their armies against Jerusalem are now the fearers of the God who defeated them. The circle closes: the psalm opened with YHWH's name being great in Israel (v1) and ends with his being feared by all earth's kings.
